triadic_ontology.findings: Two 2025 papers advance triadic-relational metaphysics. Isabelle’s “Triadic Relational Ontology” argues that after the primordial separation of “here/not-here,” meaning can only be sustained by an irreducible third term—a relational field actively maintained between poles—claiming dyadic logic and language cannot reconstruct this origin. Tricard’s March 2025 Acta Analytica article “An Ultimate Argument Against Nominalistic Relationalism” critiques the view that quantities reduce to networks of concrete relations, contending such dyadic relationalism fails to ground determinate quantities. Together they form the emerging 2025-2026 debate on whether triadic structures are required for intelligibility and quantity.

ai_systems.findings: Three 2026 arXiv papers describe next-gen autonomous research systems: DOVA introduces deliberation-first orchestration with a 3-phase hybrid reasoning pipeline and 6-tier token budgeting for 40-60% cost savings; pAI/MSc focuses on human-in-the-loop ML theory workflows that cut steering effort by orders of magnitude while keeping academic rigor; Mimosa presents an evolving multi-agent framework that auto-synthesizes task-specific workflows, achieving 43.1% success on ScienceAgentBench via iterative feedback loops and MCP-based tool discovery.

weather: Mist and cool this morning in Edmonds—49°F with 97% humidity and SSE winds at 5 mph. The day will stay overcast, topping out near 61°F, but rain is unlikely (0% chance). Skies clear tonight, dropping to about 50°F with light winds.

local_news.findings: The Edmonds City Council postponed until next week its vote on confirming Stephanie Lucash as the new city administrator. The council also advanced a proposal to "unpause" the volunteer Salary Commission, which will review and make binding recommendations on salaries for the mayor and council members. Planning Director Mike Clugston provided an update on major development projects, including the 411-unit Edmonds Green and 258-unit Apollo Apartments, and outlined plans for a streamlined detached accessory dwelling unit (DADU) permitting program.

Gayla Shoemake, a dedicated environmental advocate and long-time community volunteer, has been named the Edmonds Kiwanis Club's 2026 Citizen of the Year. The award recognizes her work with the Edmonds Climate Advisory Board, advocacy for sustainable policies, and leadership in local environmental initiatives.

The Edmonds City Council unanimously adopted the city's first-ever comprehensive Safety Action Plan, a federally required document that makes the city eligible for Safe Streets and Roads For All grants. The plan aims to eliminate traffic fatalities and serious injuries by 2040 and identifies 11 priority safety improvement projects across the city.

global_news.findings: Israel launched its first airstrike on Beirut since the April ceasefire, targeting senior Hezbollah commander Malek Balou in the southern Dahieh suburb. Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said the strike was ordered jointly with Defense Minister Israel Katz to 'neutralise' the Radwan Force leader, whom Israel accuses of orchestrating attacks on its settlements and soldiers. Lebanese Health Ministry reports four killed and several wounded; the attack marks a significant escalation after weeks of relative calm along the Israel-Lebanon frontier.

The United States and Iran are reportedly nearing a 14-point memorandum of understanding that could end the 76-day war. President Trump says a deal is 'very possible' within a 30-day window, while Iranian officials call the US proposal a 'wish list' and insist the Strait of Hormuz will remain closed until all threats cease. US forces simultaneously disabled an Iranian-flagged oil tanker in the Gulf of Oman, underscoring the fragile state of negotiations mediated by Pakistan.

China's military court handed suspended death sentences to two former defence ministers, Wei Fenghe and Li Shangfu, after convicting them of massive bribery. Both men—who successively led the PLA Rocket Force—were expelled from the Communist Party in 2024; their sentences will convert to life imprisonment without parole after a two-year reprieve. The ruling is the latest in Xi Jinping's sweeping anti-corruption purge of senior military ranks.

A hantavirus outbreak aboard a luxury cruise ship has killed three passengers and forced the medical evacuation of several others. The vessel remains at sea while health authorities in multiple countries scramble to trace and test hundreds of passengers and crew; early tests confirm Seoul virus, a particularly lethal hantavirus strain. Port calls have been cancelled and the ship has been placed under quarantine protocols.

Singapore will introduce judicial caning as a mandatory sentence for boys aged 10-16 convicted of bullying offences under amendments passed by parliament. The Ministry of Education says the measure, which follows high-profile cases of school violence, is designed to act as a strong deterrent while remaining compliant with the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child. Rights groups have condemned the move as state-sanctioned violence against minors.

intellectual_journals.findings: Sean Irving's essay 'The West's forgotten republican heritage' argues that the contemporary crisis of democratic legitimacy stems from liberals' abandonment of economic republicanism—the 19th-century tradition that tied political liberty to citizens' economic independence. Irving traces how republican thinkers from Machiavelli to Jefferson understood freedom as the absence of domination, requiring not just political rights but economic power to resist arbitrary authority. The piece revives forgotten figures like British trade unionists and land reformers who fought for shorter hours and anti-m [TRUNCATED]

In 'The One Hundred Milliseconds Between the World and You,' Maria Popova explores Oliver Sacks' neurological insights into perception as creative construction rather than passive reception. Drawing on Sacks' case studies of patients whose consciousness could 'freeze' while the world continued around them, the essay examines how neural 'coalitions' form and dissolve in milliseconds to create our subjective experience. Popova connects Sacks' work to William Blake's notion of perception as a 'cleansed door,' arguing that consciousness is not a window but a mirror reflecting our accumulated exper [TRUNCATED]

Pankaj Mishra's 'The Authority of Thought' reflects on how canonical thinkers from Arendt to Orwell illuminate our current political darkness. Writing from his Himalayan home, Mishra describes re-reading classic critiques of imperialism and totalitarianism while processing news of US intervention in Venezuela and Western complicity in genocide. The essay argues that the liberal-democratic order's collapse reveals not just hypocrisy but the fundamental dependence of Western power on unacknowledged structures of domination—structures that thinkers like Mark Twain and George Orwell exposed in ear [TRUNCATED]

The Spring 2026 issue's essay 'The Death of Asylum' by Tanvi Misra traces how centuries of efforts to deny refuge to persecuted people created the intellectual scaffolding for contemporary authoritarianism. Through historical analysis of asylum law from medieval sanctuary practices to modern refugee regimes, Misra demonstrates how ostensibly humanitarian frameworks were designed to exclude—establishing precedents now weaponized by anti-immigrant movements worldwide. The piece connects this genealogy to current debates about Palestinian refugees and the global crackdown on asylum rights.

Gideon Lewis-Kraus' 'What Is Claude? Anthropic Doesn't Know, Either' examines the fundamental opacity of large language models through the case of Claude's inexplicable fixation on bananas. The piece reveals how even Claude's creators cannot fully explain why the model consistently steers conversations toward bananas, illustrating broader epistemological problems with AI systems whose decision-making processes remain inscrutable. Through interviews with Anthropic researchers, the essay explores the tension between AI capabilities and our inability to understand their internal logic—raising que [TRUNCATED]

wearable_ai.findings: Voice-first AI hardware has crystallized into three distinct form factors in 2026. **Friend pendant** (friend.com, $7.9M raised) remains the category leader with 6 employees and 257% YoY growth, shipping as a discreet neck-worn companion that listens continuously and texts contextual insights. **Audria** (audria.tech, 7 employees) takes a different approach as a behind-the-ear device focused on pr [TRUNCATED]

English and History teachers gained four specialized AI co-workers this spring. **MagicSchool AI** (magicschool.ai, 224 employees, $62.4M raised) leads with 7M+ educators using its platform for lesson planning, assessments, and IEP writing across 16 countries. **Brisk Teaching** (briskteaching.com, 74 employees, $21.9M raised) offers a Chrome extension that overlays existing teacher tools, providi [TRUNCATED]

The lifelogging pendant category has evolved into two distinct camps in 2026. **Nirva** (nirva.life, 4 employees) represents the jewelry-first approach with its AI jewelry that journals your life, building a 'living library of your life' as the memory layer for downstream AI apps, having won 6 awards at CES 2026. **Omi** (omi.me, 17 employees, $2.7M raised) takes the productivity-focused route, ca [TRUNCATED]

newyorker.title: Colbert’s Trumpet Player on Life After Late Night
newyorker.dek: Since 2015, Jon Lampley has played in the house band of “The Late Show,” which CBS unceremoniously cancelled. As the final episode looms, he takes a look back. Mark Yarm writes.
newyorker.text:
Viewers across the country are counting down the days till the final episode of Stephen Colbert’s “Late Show,” which will air on May 21st. When the program’s peculiar, sudden cancellation was announced, last July, the members of the show’s band, Louis Cato and the Great Big Joy Machine, were as surprised as anyone. “It felt like a deep sigh,” Jon (Lamps) Lampley, the group’s trumpet player, recalled the other day, at a sushi place near the Ed Sullivan Theatre.
CBS defended the cancellation as “purely a financial decision.” But many linked it to Colbert’s criticism of the network’s parent company for paying sixteen million dollars to settle a lawsuit with President Donald Trump—a move that the host decried, on air, as a “big fat bribe.” Lampley, who is thirty-six and wears a neat beard, said, “I don’t really have a lot of interest in fuelling speculation.” He is unhappy, though, about how things went down.
Colbert already has his next gig lined up: writing a “Lord of the Rings” movie with his son Peter McGee and Philippa Boyens. Lampley, meanwhile, will release his second solo album in June—a joyous soul-pop record called “Notes to Self,” whose lead single, “Greener,” features a spoken-word cameo from Colbert. (“Hey, Lamps, how’s your day goin’?”) On the album, the trumpeter levels up as a singer and front man. “It was influenced by the gospel music that’s at the core of what I do,” he said.
Lampley grew up in the predominantly white suburb of Tallmadge, Ohio, but he attended the First Apostolic Faith Church, a Black, Pentecostal congregation in Akron. In seventh grade, he joined the church band. He rattled off the names of various sisters and elders he performed alongside. “Nobody knows who these people are, but they are the ones who taught me, This is what music is,” he said. “This is how you move people. This is how you play the guitar in a way that gets an entire church to clap their hands.” Lampley maintains a deep faith.
He went on to play sousaphone in the Ohio State University marching band, where, as a senior, he experienced one of his proudest moments: “dotting the i” in an on-field cursive formation of the word “Ohio.” “This is considered a very great honor,” he said. During college, he also co-founded a jazz-funk band now known as the Huntertones. In 2014, the band moved to New York, where Lampley connected with the keyboardist and singer Jon Batiste, who hired him for the band he was assembling for Colbert. Batiste left the show in 2022, and the musician Louis Cato was promoted to bandleader.
“I can’t believe that I chose to make my life out of, like, blowing through a metal pipe,” Lampley said. “The Late Show” was taping that afternoon, and he made his way to the theatre for wardrobe, hair, and makeup. Later, between Colbert’s interviews with Anne Hathaway, who zinged her “Devil Wears Prada” co-star Stanley Tucci about his foodie persona (“That bitch has not made me a morsel!”), and the comedian Josh Johnson, who recounted an amusing T.S.A. encounter (“They open my bag, and it wasn’t brass knuckles—it was toe spreaders”), Lampley showed off his chops, soloing on a rendition of the Steve Miller Band’s “Fly Like an Eagle.”
At the taping, Colbert, hands in pockets, addressed his trumpet player. “Lampley, I want you to know that a friend of mine came to the show last night,” he said. “And she said, ‘Wooo, that trumpet player.’ ” Colbert grinned. “She goes, ‘If I wasn’t happily married, he’d be in big trouble.’ ” The audience whooped.
“You know what it is?” Cato interjected. “It’s the hips.”
“I think that’s it,” Colbert said. “Or it’s the embouchure.”
“Or it’s both,” Lampley said. He illustrated his lipping technique.
“You’re always, like, pre-kissing,” Colbert observed.
Afterward, Lampley and the band’s saxophonist, Louis Fouché, headed to an Italian restaurant. Lampley brought up the exchange involving Colbert’s friend, which ended up being cut before air. “Man,” he said, “I was grateful that my girlfriend was not at the taping today.”
The impending end of the Colbert era was weighing on them all. “Somebody who has been that important to the game and to the craft deserves to go out on their own terms,” Lampley said. “And that was taken from Stephen.” ♦

literary_pick.title: The Known World
literary_pick.author: Edward P. Jones
literary_pick.summary: Winner of the 2004 Pulitzer Prize and hailed by the New York Times as the best book of fiction of the 21st century, The Known World is a debut novel of stunning emotional depth and literary power. Set in antebellum Virginia, it tells the story of Henry Townsend, a former slave who becomes proprietor of his own plantation—and his own slaves. After his death, the plantation begins to unravel as slaves escape, families betray one another, and the broader world of slavery and freedom begins to collapse. The novel weaves together the lives of the freed and the enslaved, offering a profound and mult [TRUNCATED]

enriched_articles.title: The West's forgotten republican heritage | Aeon Essays
enriched_articles.text: Across the democratic world, citizens describe a growing sense of powerlessness. Governments rise and fall, but the power to shape daily life now flows elsewhere – through markets, corporations and data systems untouched by democratic oversight. A recent international survey found that, in almost every major country, most people believe the economy is rigged to benefit the rich and powerful; many say their societies are 'broken'. Discontent has fuelled a turn toward authoritarian politics, which

enriched_articles.title: The Authority of Thought, by Pankaj Mishra
enriched_articles.text: "Even in the darkest of times," wrote Hannah Arendt, "we have the right to expect some illumination." The words are prominent on the back cover of an early Pelican edition of her book Men in Dark Times, which I noticed on one of the crammed shelves in my Himalayan home one calamitous morning of the New Year. I had just recoiled from a New York Times headline: a defense of u.s. intervention in venezuela. The subhead was more horrifying still: the former envoy elliott abrams says the administratio

enriched_articles.title: Iran considering US proposal as Trump says war will be 'over quickly'
enriched_articles.text: US President Donald Trump has predicted that the war in Iran will be "over quickly" and says that most people "understand" his goal of ending Tehran's nuclear ambitions. His comments come after Iran said a US proposal to end the war is "still being considered". US news outlet Axios reported that the White House believes it could be closing in on a 14-point memorandum of understanding with Iran, which could set a framework for more detailed nuclear negotiations.

enriched_articles.title: An Ultimate Argument Against Nominalistic Relationalism | Acta Analytica | Springer Nature Link
enriched_articles.text: Relationalism is the view that a quantity (i.e., space or mass) consists only of a network of concrete objects that stand in determinate relations (spatial relations or mass-relations). At its very core, the theory claims that an object possesses a determinate quantity fundamentally by standing in determinate relations in that quantity to other objects. For instance, my laptop's mass consists fundamentally in the fact that it is k times more or less massive than other objects (my neighbor's car,

enriched_articles.title: Oliver Sacks on Perception, the Paradox of Mental Illness, and How the Brain Sees the World
enriched_articles.text: In 1996, neurologist Oliver Sacks wrote that "the brain is a machine for jumping to conclusions" — a prescient observation that anticipated our modern understanding of how perception works. In his posthumously published essays, Sacks explores how the brain constructs reality through a complex interplay of sensory input, memory, and expectation. He argues that what we call "mental illness" may actually be different ways of perceiving the world, rather than simply defects in brain function.
